original_abstract: information about the image, i.e., it covers whole image areas instead of pixel-level information. Thereby, the image is principally accessible to advanced applications such as predicting future image appearances irrespective of image rotation or ­translation2. The contributions of our study are: (i) We present a method that can generate high-resolution 3D images by learning the structure in the latent space of a VQ-GAN, thereby reducing computational requirements. (ii) The versatility of this method is demonstrated on four publicly available datasets of various modalities and anatomic regions with limited dataset size (n ≤ 1844 images) and we make our code publicly available for everyone to use. (iii) Our model outperforms a well-established reference GAN by generating more diverse images less prone to mode collapse. (iv) Established metrics for quality assessment of synthetic data may fail to assess images like ­humans21. To overcome the limitations of metric-based evaluations, we improve the validity and relevance of these quality assessments by having experienced radiologists assess the quality of the generated images. (v) We demonstrate the potential of the synthetic data generated by our model in low-data settings. By pre-training a Swin-UNETR model in a self-supervised fashion with synthetic images of one institution, we find that this pre-trained model is a good candidate for downstream tasks (such as segmentation) in other institutions. Con- sequently, the model’s performance is largely improved in low-data regimes compared to models with no pre- training with synthetic data. This finding is particularly relevant in medical imaging, where datasets are typically limited in size.
humanized_abstract: Description of the image content, meaning that large image portions (beyond the single pixel level) are exposed to deep image analysis tasks. Such image analysis tasks include, but are not limited to, prediction of image appearances of the future or after rotating/translate the current image2. In this work, the following contributions are made: 1. We proposed a method that leverages VQ-GAN for producing 3D high resolution images and at the same time reducing the running time of the method, that is located in the latent space. 2. We evaluated our method using four different public data-sets of various medical image modalities and anatomic locations, with relatively low in number (from n = 1844, down to n = 30). Our implementation and the source code can be downloaded from: https://github.com/seyedali88/SGVI 3. That the generated images from our SGVI-based method significantly outperform those produced from an established state-of-the-art GAN, due to the significantly higher sample diversity and lower mode-caching. 4. Image quality assessment metrics used on synthesized data are less representative of how humans judge it. Therefore, including a radiologist evaluation of synthetic images significantly improves the reliability of the image quality evaluation. 5. Validity of our method by using synthesized images produced from our SGVI method to pre-train in the self-supervised mode the Swin-UNETR model of a particular data-set of images originating from a certain hospital, yielding the most accurate and competitive results when the pre-trained model is used on another data-set from a different hospital and that significantly outperforms the non-pre-trained version, primarily for low-data settings. A setting highly relevant in medical imaging.
